# M12 · Valuation-Aware Entry

!!! abstract "Learning objectives"
    By the end of this module you will be able to:

    - Read the core **market-valuation signals** — P/E, P/B, dividend yield, **earnings yield vs bond yield (the yield gap)**, and **market-cap-to-GDP**.
    - Explain why high starting valuations lower *expected* forward returns — and why this sets the **odds, not the timing**.
    - Convert valuation into a **deployment-pace** decision (lump vs tranched STP) rather than a market-timing bet.
    - Decompose **expected return** into earnings growth + dividend yield ± valuation change.

This is the quantitative upgrade of **M6's** "when to enter". It uses the risk vocabulary of **M9–M11** and feeds rebalancing ([**M13**](m13-monitoring-rebalancing.md)) and construction ([**M15**](m15-portfolio-construction.md)).

!!! warning "Not a market call"
    Nothing here predicts the market or recommends buying/selling at a moment. Valuation shifts *probabilities over years*; it is useless as a short-term timer. Figures are illustrative.

---

## 1. Intuition first — valuation sets the odds, not the date

Markets are not a coin flip independent of price. **What you pay shapes what you earn** — buy when the market is expensive and your *expected* multi-year return is lower; buy when it's cheap and the odds tilt your way. But — and this is the whole discipline — **valuation tells you nothing about *when*.** An expensive market can get more expensive for years. So a professional doesn't *time* entry on valuation; they adjust the **pace** of deployment and the **expectations** they set.

![Schematic: higher starting P/E tends to mean lower forward return](data:image/png;base64,)

*Schematic (not a forecast): each dot is a hypothetical starting valuation and the return that followed. The cloud slopes down — higher starting P/E tends to precede lower multi-year returns — but the **scatter is wide**, which is exactly why valuation governs *odds and pace*, not a precise entry date.*

---

## 2. The core valuation signals

!!! note "Definitions — the market gauges"
    - **P/E (price-to-earnings)** — price per ₹1 of earnings. High = expensive (or high growth priced in).
    - **Earnings yield** = **1 / P/E** — the earnings you "buy" per ₹100 invested; directly comparable to a bond yield.
    - **P/B (price-to-book)** — price per ₹1 of net assets; useful where earnings are cyclical (banks).
    - **Dividend yield** — income per ₹100; low yield often accompanies rich valuations.
    - **Market-cap-to-GDP ("Buffett indicator")** — total market value vs the economy's size; a long-run rich/cheap gauge.

### 2.1 The yield gap — equities vs bonds

The most decision-useful signal compares the **earnings yield** of equities with the **government-bond yield**:

!!! note "Formula — the yield gap (equity risk premium proxy)"
    $$\text{Yield gap} = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{P/E}}_{\text{equity earnings yield}} - \underbrace{R_{\text{10y G-sec}}}_{\text{bond yield}}$$
    A *positive, wide* gap means equities are relatively cheap vs bonds; a *thin or negative* gap means equities are richly priced relative to the safe alternative.

### Worked example 1 — reading the yield gap

The Nifty trades at a trailing **P/E of 22** → earnings yield = 1/22 = **4.55%**. The 10-year G-sec yields **7.0%**.
$$\text{Yield gap} = 4.55\% - 7.0\% = -2.45\%$$
Equities are yielding *less* than the risk-free bond — a **rich** reading. The response is **not** "sell" or "don't invest"; it is **"deploy fresh lump sums more slowly, set lower return expectations, and keep rebalancing."** Contrast: at a P/E of 15, earnings yield = 6.67%, gap = −0.33% — far less stretched, so deploy faster.

---

## 3. From valuation to deployment pace

The practical output of valuation analysis is **how fast to put a lump sum to work** — a direct upgrade of the STP idea from [**M6**](m06-lifecycle-decisions.md).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V{"Where is the market<br/>vs its own history?"}
    V -- "Cheap band<br/>(low P/E, wide yield gap)" --> C["Deploy faster<br/>(lump or short 3–6m STP)"]
    V -- "Fair band" --> F["Normal SIP / 6–9m STP"]
    V -- "Expensive band<br/>(high P/E, thin/neg gap)" --> E["Stagger slowly<br/>(12–24m STP, more in liquid)"]
    classDef c fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20;
    classDef e fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c;
    class C c;
    class E e;
```

### Worked example 2 — staggering ₹12,00,000 by valuation

You have **₹12,00,000** to deploy with the market in an **expensive** band (Example 1). Instead of a lump sum, you park it in a liquid fund and run a **valuation-aware STP** over **18 months** (₹66,667/month), with a rule to **accelerate** transfers if the market falls into the fair/cheap band. Outcomes:

- If the market **corrects**, you deploy the rest *faster and cheaper* — valuation rewarded your patience.
- If it **keeps rising**, you gave up some upside on the parked balance (which still earned ~6–7% in liquid) — the price of insurance against entering at a peak.

The point is **not** to predict which happens, but to **size the regret**: when valuations are stretched, the cost of being wrong about a lump sum is highest, so you stagger.

---

## 4. Decomposing expected return (setting honest expectations)

!!! note "Formula — return decomposition"
    $$\text{Expected return} \approx \underbrace{\text{earnings growth}}_{\text{fundamentals}} + \underbrace{\text{dividend yield}}_{\text{income}} \pm \underbrace{\text{valuation change}}_{\text{re-rating}}$$

### Worked example 3 — where future return must come from

At a P/E of 22, suppose earnings grow **12%/yr** and dividend yield is **1.3%**. If valuations *hold*, expected return ≈ 13.3%. But if the P/E **mean-reverts** to 18 over five years, that's a valuation drag of roughly **−4%/yr** of multiple compression, cutting expected return to ~**9%**. This is why entering at high valuations lowers expected returns *mechanically* — you are relying on earnings to outrun a shrinking multiple. Setting this expectation up front is what keeps an investor from panic when a richly-bought market merely *normalises*.

---

## 5. How Balanced Advantage Funds automate this

**Dynamic Asset Allocation / Balanced Advantage Funds (BAFs)** (M3) operationalise valuation-aware entry *inside a single fund*: a model raises equity when valuations (P/E, P/B, yield gap) are low and trims it when they're high. For an investor who won't run their own STP discipline, a BAF outsources the pace decision — at the cost of trusting the AMC's model and a higher TER than a plain index fund.

---

## 6. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts

!!! danger "Valuation traps"
    1. **Using valuation as a short-term timer.** Expensive markets can stay expensive for years; cheap can get cheaper.
    2. **Stopping SIPs because "the market is high".** SIPs are horizon tools ([**M6**](m06-lifecycle-decisions.md)); valuation adjusts *lump-sum* pace, not your monthly discipline.
    3. **Single-metric tunnel vision.** A high P/E can be justified by a rate cut or earnings surge; read the **yield gap and growth**, not P/E alone.
    4. **"This time is different" at the top, or refusing to invest at all at the bottom.**
    5. **Comparing P/E across markets/sectors** without adjusting for growth and composition.

!!! success "Do"
    - **Do** let valuation set **deployment pace and expectations**, not entry dates.
    - **Do** read the **yield gap** alongside P/E, and keep SIPs running regardless.
    - **Do** stagger lump sums more when valuations are stretched; consider a **BAF** to automate it.

!!! failure "Don't"
    - **Don't** try to call tops and bottoms.
    - **Don't** abandon a long-horizon plan over a valuation reading.

---

## 7. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026

- **Dynamic Asset Allocation / BAF category** — the regulated vehicle that implements valuation-based equity shifting ([**M3**](m03-taxonomy.md)). *[verify section no.]*
- **Riskometer & disclosures** — risk labelling that should accompany any valuation-driven product. *[verify]*
- **Performance-claim / advertising norms** — restrictions on implying market-timing ability or guaranteed returns. *[verify]*
- Valuation analysis itself is an **analytical discipline**, not a regulation; SEBI governs the *products and disclosures* around it.

---

## 8. Key takeaways

!!! quote "Key takeaways"
    - **What you pay shapes what you earn** — high starting valuations lower *expected* multi-year returns.
    - Valuation sets the **odds and the pace**, never the **date** — it is a poor short-term timer.
    - Read the **yield gap** (earnings yield − bond yield) with P/E, P/B, dividend yield and market-cap/GDP.
    - Convert valuation into **deployment pace**: deploy faster when cheap, **stagger via STP** when expensive; keep SIPs running.
    - Decompose expected return into **earnings growth + dividend ± valuation change** to set honest expectations; **BAFs** automate the pace.

---

## 9. A word from the field

!!! quote "On price versus value"
    *"Price is what you pay; value is what you get."*

    — **Warren Buffett** (echoing his teacher Benjamin Graham). Valuation-aware entry is simply taking this seriously: the *price level* of the market when you deploy is one of the few things you can observe in advance, so let it govern your **pace and expectations** — without pretending it tells you the day.
